steps.title: Acute rheumatic fever & rheumatic heart disease
steps.text: A 10-year-old child presents at a health centre with fever, swollen joints, and a history of sore throat 3 weeks ago. On examination, there is a new heart murmur. What is the most appropriate immediate action at the primary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At the primary care level, the correct action is to stabilize the child, administer a stat dose of IM benzathine penicillin, and refer. Starting ibuprofen or delaying referral may miss the window for specialist care. Referral without penicillin risks progression of infection. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start ibuprofen and oral penicillin, monitor for 48 hours, then refer if no improvement
Give a stat IM dose of benzathine penicillin, start ibuprofen, and refer the patient
Stabilize the patient, give a stat dose of IM benzathine penicillin, and refer to a higher-level facility
Refer immediately to tertiary care without giving treatment to avoid masking symptoms

steps.title: Myocarditis
steps.text: What is the most appropriate management step for suspected myocarditis at the primary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the guideline recommends stabilizing the patient and referring — definitive diagnosis and treatment occur at higher levels. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start corticosteroids immediately
Order an echocardiogram and monitor at the facility
Stabilize the patient and refer to a higher-level facility
Begin long-term heart failure medications

steps.title: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
steps.text: What is the correct management approach for a child presenting with signs suggestive of dilated cardiomyopathy at the primary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the key responsibility is to stabilize the child and refer for further evaluation and treatment. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start long-term heart failure medication and monitor
Administer corticosteroids and observe
Stabilize the patient and refer to higher-level care
Delay intervention until echocardiography is available

steps.title: Pulmonary hypertension
steps.text: What is the most appropriate course of action at the primary care level when a child is suspected to have pulmonary hypertension? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the focus is on initial stabilization and referral to a higher-level facility for specialist input. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start vasodilators and observe response
Begin diuretics and discharge if improved
Stabilize the patient and refer for pulmonologist and/or cardiologist review
Delay referral until heart failure develops

steps.title: Congenital Heart diseases
steps.text: What is the appropriate course of action at the primary care level for a neonate suspected to have congenital heart disease? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At primary care level, the role is to stabilize the patient using ABCDE and then refer for further evaluation. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start heart failure medications and monitor
Perform chest X-ray and defer referral
Stabilize using ABCDE approach and refer to secondary care
Begin CPAP and provide long-term follow-up

steps.title: Hypercyanotic Spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
steps.text: Why is a child with a hypercyanotic spell placed in a knee–chest position or held on the caregiver’s shoulder with knees tucked? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: This positioning helps manage a Tet spell by reducing systemic venous return (decreasing preload) and increasing systemic vascular resistance, which improves pulmonary blood flow and oxygenation. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: To reduce pulmonary pressure and increase heart rate
To calm the child and improve airway patency
To increase systemic venous return and increase systemic vascular resistance
To promote deeper breathing and increase oxygen uptake

steps.title: Hypercyanotic Spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
steps.text: A child with Tetralogy of Fallot presents with a cyanotic spell. Which of the following interventions can be performed at the primary level to support stabilization? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Key interventions at the primary level include positioning, oxygen, and fluid bolus to improve preload and systemic resistance. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: IV propranolol and morphine
Knee–chest position, 100% oxygen, and fluid bolus (RL or NS 20 ml/kg)
Transfer immediately without intervention
Administer corticosteroids and diuretics

steps.title: Hypercyanotic Spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
steps.text: A child with Tetralogy of Fallot is having a hypercyanotic spell and remains poorly perfused after initial stabilization. According to the guideline, how should fluid resuscitation be administered at the primary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The guideline advises giving a 20 ml/kg bolus of RL or NS over 30 minutes, with a maximum of 2 boluses if the child does not improve. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: 10 ml/kg over 1 hour, repeat once if needed
20 ml/kg rapid IV bolus over 30 minutes, repeat once if no improvement (maximum 2 boluses)
Continuous maintenance fluids only
30 ml/kg over 15 minutes, repeat up to 3 times

steps.title: Congestive Heart Failure
steps.text: A 1-year-old presents with signs of congestive heart failure. What is the most appropriate action at the primary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the guideline advises stabilization (ABCDE), giving furosemide 1 mg/kg stat if available (IV or oral), and referral to secondary care. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start long-term diuretics and monitor for 72 hours
Stabilize using ABCDE, give a stat dose of furosemide if available
Begin oxygen and corticosteroids, then discharge
Refer immediately without providing any treatment

steps.title: Paediatric Arrhythimias
steps.text: A 7-year-old presents to a primary care facility with altered consciousness and a heart rate over 220 bpm. The child appears pale, cold, and poorly perfused. What is the most appropriate action? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: This is an unstable arrhythmia. At the primary level, the correct response is to stabilize using ABCs and refer immediately to tertiary care. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Administer oral beta-blocker and monitor for 2 hours
Perform a 12-lead ECG and refer the next day
Begin ABC stabilization and urgently refer to tertiary care
Attempt vagal maneuvers and discharge if heart rate improves

steps.title: Supraventricular Tachycardia
steps.text: A 5-year-old presents with a rapid heart rate (>220 bpm), is alert but appears fatigued. What is the appropriate approach at the primary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the role is to stabilize the patient using the ABCDE approach and consult or refer for further evaluation and management. Medication and advanced interventions are handled at higher levels. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Administer IV adenosine immediately
Perform synchronized cardioversion
Stabilize using ABCDE approach and consult/refer to secondary care
Start oral beta-blockers and monitor for 12 hours

steps.title: Chest Pain
steps.text: Which aspect of history is most important to assess in a child presenting with chest pain? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: A detailed description of the pain’s characteristics is essential to guide diagnosis and determine urgency of referral. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Duration, intensity, location, and radiation of pain
Family history of asthma or eczema
Recent antibiotic use
Sleep quality and school performance

steps.title: Chest Pain
steps.text: According to primary care guidelines, which of the following warrants referral of a child with chest pain to a secondary facility? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Exertional chest pain and abnormal cardiac findings may indicate a cardiac cause and should prompt referral. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Chest pain that worsens with deep breathing
Tenderness on palpation of the chest wall
Occasional complaints of mild pain at rest
Pain worsened by physical activity or abnormal cardiac exam

steps.title: Syncope
steps.text: A child presents with a brief loss of consciousness but has a normal exam and vitals. What is the most appropriate action at the primary or secondary level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Syncope may have cardiac or neurological causes. Evaluation by a cardiologist or neurologist at a tertiary center is advised. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Discharge without follow-up
Begin oral rehydration therapy
Refer to a tertiary facility for cardiology or neurology evaluation
Order a 24-hour EEG before deciding next steps
